moveAnnotations.annotation: We're stepping into the Polish Opening, an unusual first move that immediately signals we want to control the long diagonal and prepare rapid queenside expansion. While unconventional, b4 stakes a claim on c5 and supports future moves like Bb2, giving us a flexible setup that avoids the main-line theory that most players study. The move suits aggressive, creative players who prefer fighting on their own terms rather than entering heavily analyzed positions.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: b4 stakes a claim on c5 and supports Bb2, avoiding main-line theory for aggressive, creative play.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black chooses a solid, restrained response, preparing to develop the kingside while keeping central tension flexible. By playing e6 instead of immediately challenging b4, Black allows us to continue our queenside initiative but maintains the option to strike in the center later with moves like d5 or c5. This move is characteristic of French-like setups where Black prioritizes solid structure over rapid piece development.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's e6 keeps central tension flexible, allowing a later d5 or c5 strike while preserving solid structure.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Now we fulfill the main idea of b4—developing our bishop to the long diagonal where it exerts pressure on e5, f4, and importantly, supports our expansion. Bb2 is flexible and doesn't commit us to a specific pawn structure; we can later push b5 or reroute if needed. This bishop placement is the thematic feature of the Polish Opening, giving us long-range influence while keeping our position coordinated.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Bb2 develops the bishop to the long diagonal, pressuring e5 and f4 while supporting future b5 expansion.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops actively, attacking e4 and preparing to support the center or kingside. Nf6 is a developing choice that doesn't commit Black to a specific plan yet, keeping options open for both central breaks (like d5) and kingside castling. The knight on f6 is well-placed to support future play on any part of the board.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Nf6 develops actively, attacks e4, and keeps options open for d5 or kingside castling.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We push forward aggressively, attacking Black's space and forcing a decision about how to handle our advanced pawns. By playing b5, we're committing to queenside pressure and preparing to create weaknesses on Black's queenside; the move also supports a potential c4 advance later. This is the critical moment where we're claiming space and not allowing Black to sit comfortably.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: b5 commits to queenside pressure, supporting c4 and forcing Black to react to advanced pawns.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black attacks our b5 pawn, forcing us to make a critical choice about our pawn structure. Rather than retreat, a6 invites us to capture, which will lead to the unusual Rooks Swap line where material gets traded off rapidly. This move shows Black playing actively to simplify the position and reduce our attacking potential.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Capturing on b5 with a6 leads to the Rooks Swap line, rapidly trading material and reducing White's attack.

moveAnnotations.annotation: White advances the a-pawn to support the b5 outpost and threaten Black's a6 pawn, forcing a critical decision on whether to allow further queenside pressure.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: a4 is theory in the Polish Opening Rooks Swap line.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black captures on b5, accepting the trade and simplifying the queenside while keeping the position structurally balanced.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: axb5 is the theory move in the Polish Opening Rooks Swap line.

moveAnnotations.annotation: White recaptures with the a-pawn, maintaining a strong pawn on b5 and controlling key central files while keeping material equal.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: axb5 is the theory move in the Polish Opening Rooks Swap Line.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black exchanges rooks immediately, eliminating White's potential attacking piece and steering toward a quieter position with queens still on.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Rxa1 is the theory move in this Polish Opening line.

moveAnnotations.annotation: White recaptures with the bishop, placing it actively on a1 and completing the characteristic rook trade that defines this line's strategic framework.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Bxa1 is the theory move in the Polish Opening Rooks Swap Line.
